elements.sent-2411-001: Ultra-high energy (UHE) neutrinos, above 10~PeV, can be detected in dense radio-frequency (RF) transparent media through the Askaryan effect~\cite{Askaryan:1961pfb .}
elements.sent-2411-002: Cold glacial ice, available in the polar regions with the necessary abundant volumes, has been measured to have exceptional RF properties~\cite{Barwick_2005 and continues to be the best detector medium for measuring these UHE particles nearly 40~years from the inception of the experimental concept~\cite{MARKOV1986242 .}}
elements.sent-2411-003: Experimental efforts towards large-scale in-ice radio detectors have so far been focused in Antarctica, through the RICE, ARA, and ARIANNA projects~\cite{Kravchenko:2011im,Allison:2011wk,Anker:2019rzo .}
elements.sent-2411-004: There has yet to be a detection of UHE neutrinos with these radio instruments, though the continued technology advancement, simulation maturity, and field deployment experiences have provided a valuable knowledge base to scale up the experimental design to the requisite detector volumes.
elements.sent-2411-005: The Greenlandic ice sheet was first proposed as a suitable location for a UHE neutrino radio detector with measurements of the RF attenuation length near Summit Station in 2013~\cite{Avva:2014ena and then a conceptual experimental design known as the Greenland Neutrino Observatory (GNO) that presented early field testing results~\cite{Avva:2016ggs .}}
elements.sent-2411-006: With GNO on indefinite hiatus, a new experimental effort to build a large-scale radio array reemerged in the form of the Radio Neutrino Observatory Greenland (RNO-G), which was first described in a 2020 whitepaper~\cite{RNO-G:2020rmc .}
elements.sent-2411-007: RNO-G followed the initial RF attenuation length measurements with a detailed study in 2021, confirming the suitability of the Greenland ice sheet as a radio-detection volume for UHE neutrinos with a measured attenuation length of $\sim$1~km at 200~MHz~\cite{Aguilar:2022kgi .}
elements.sent-2411-008: Neutrino-induced particle cascades in dense media, such as ice, emit coherent broadband radio waves via the Askaryan effect, which describes the charge-excess nature of such cascades~\cite{Askaryan:1961pfb .}
elements.sent-2411-009: The high-energy particle shower develops a negative charge excess, which coherently emits radio over a broadband frequency spectrum, and for high-energy extended tracks, the radio power is beamed in a relatively narrow angular range around a conical section defined by the Cherenkov angle.
elements.sent-2411-010: This emission mechanism has been confirmed in a set of accelerator based measurements using a variety of dense media, including ice~\cite{ANITA:2006nif,T-510:2015pyu,Bechtol:2021tyd .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Thus, the neutrino signature at the radio receiver, within the RNO-G band of $\sim$100-700~MHz, is a nanosecond-scale electric field impulse with Cherenkov-like characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-012: The radio-wave polarization of the electric field points radially inwards towards the shower axis.
elements.sent-2411-013: The science case of RNO-G, namely the discovery and measurement of neutrinos above 10~PeV in energy, has been extensively discussed in recent comprehensive reviews, i.e.~\cite{Ackermann:2022rqc, MammenAbraham:2022xoc, Ackermann:2019ows,Ackermann:2019cxh .}
elements.sent-2411-014: A conclusive picture emerges that predicts a potentially detectable flux of neutrinos, which is a result of the interaction of ultra-high energy cosmic rays and various photon backgrounds in the universe.
elements.sent-2411-015: RNO-G aims to improve existing limits \cite{ANITA:2019wyx,ARA:2019wcf,Anker:2019rzo,IceCube:2018fhm,PierreAuger:2015aqe on the neutrino flux, if not discover the flux of EeV neutrinos.}
elements.sent-2411-016: This article reports on the design and performance of the first seven stations of RNO-G, which is planned to consist of 35 stations when the first phase of construction is complete.
elements.sent-2411-017: Each RNO-G station comprises 24-antenna receivers installed down to a depth of 100~m.
elements.sent-2411-018: The RNO-G work effort began in earnest in mid-2019, when design and construction of 10~full RNO-G stations of hardware components was initiated, followed by field deployments to Greenland in 2021 and 2022 during which time the initial seven stations (RNO-G-7) were installed and commissioned.
elements.sent-2411-019: After these first field seasons, and affected by the global chip shortage, RNO-G paused deployment in 2023 in order to evaluate the instrument performance and design aspects before pushing forward to complete the array.
elements.sent-2411-020: This article will describe these in detail, with the experimental overview including drilling in \autoref{sec:experiment , the instrument design in \autoref{sec:instrument , and a review of the initial experiment performance in \autoref{sec:performance .}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: RNO-G is a radio array composed of independent stations, which are designed to autonomously detect and record transient signals.
elements.sent-2411-022: The array is currently under construction adjacent to Summit Station near the top of the Greenlandic ice sheet.
elements.sent-2411-023: Summit Station is operated by the National Science Foundation (NSF) and provides the deployment base-camp and communications hub for the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-024: The RNO-G design requires a scalable and robust design approach such that the stations can be efficiently deployed and can operate within a small power envelope to run from renewable-energy power sources.
elements.sent-2411-025: The experiment is enabled by a wide range of custom-designed low-power radio-frequency (RF), digital, and power electronics that were developed at an expeditious pace to meet the deployment goals for the 2021 and 2022 field seasons.
elements.sent-2411-026: Additionally, a new drill developed by British Antarctic Survey (BAS) is used to make the \SI{100 {m boreholes for the stations.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: In this section, we discuss the overall array plan, the RNO-G station layout, and the drilling aspects of the project.
elements.sent-2411-028: The array layout as planned for the first 35 stations is shown in \autoref{fig:array_and_station_layout .}
elements.sent-2411-029: The square grid has an inter-station spacing of \SI{1.25 {km , which was chosen so that the effective volume of each station is independent at the lowest energies and will lead to inter-station coincident measurements at the 10\% level for neutrino interactions at EeV energies.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: This is driven mostly by the attenuation length \cite{Aguilar:2022kgi,Aguilar:2022ijn and the refractive index profile of the ice \cite{Deaconu:2018bkf,Aguilar:2023udv .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: The stations are denoted by a grid numbering scheme (x,y) and, colloquially, by fauna endemic to Greenland, using the local language kalaallisut.
elements.sent-2411-032: The first seven installed stations of RNO-G are Nanoq (Polar Bear, 11), Terianniaq (Arctic Fox, 12), Ukaleq (Arctic Hare, 13), Amaroq (Arctic Wolf, 21), Avinngaq (Arctic Lemming, 22), Ukaliatsiaq (Stoat, 23), and Qappik (Wolverine, 24).
elements.sent-2411-033: The location of the array with respect to Summit Camp proper and the skiway is restricted by the requirements of aviation safety, the clean air and snow sectors to the west and south, as well as the IceSat-2 protected area to the north-east \cite{Kelly2017 .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.64\textwidth]{figures/simple-print-inst-paper.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.35\textwidth]{figures/RNO-G_station.png} \caption{Left: Planned and existing layout of the 35 station array of RNO-G. The first seven stations, the subject of this paper, were deployed in 2021 and 2022. The main building of Summit Station is labeled Big House. Right: The station construction as installed for the first seven stations of RNO-G, showing the three instrumented boreholes down to \SI{100}{m}, as well as the antennas in shallow trenches near the surface.} \label{fig:array_and_station_layout}
elements.sent-2411-034: The layout of the first seven RNO-G stations is shown on the right in \autoref{fig:array_and_station_layout .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Each station comprises 24 low-noise receiving antennas installed in a hybrid configuration with both near-surface and deep receivers.
elements.sent-2411-036: The radio signals are received at antennas installed at different depths as shown in the right panel of \autoref{fig:array_and_station_layout .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Because of the increasing density of the ice with depth within the firn ($\sim$80~m near Summit), radio waves from a deep source, such as the Askaryan-generated emission from an UHE neutrino, propagate along curved ray paths as they approach the surface so that antennas deployed deeper in the ice are sensitive to a larger volume of ice.
elements.sent-2411-038: Antennas that are installed closer to the surface, while contributing less to the overall neutrino sensitivity, can be used to tag potential natural or human-made backgrounds originating above the ice.
elements.sent-2411-039: The linear polarization angle of the Askaryan signal can be used to reconstruct the neutrino arrival direction and so it is beneficial to measure both the vertical and horizontal component of the electric field with the borehole antennas.
elements.sent-2411-040: The deep antennas are deployed in three boreholes down to 100 meters depth.
elements.sent-2411-041: Due to the signal propagation, the main neutrino effective volume arises from the closely packed triggering subarray of four antennas at the bottom of what is called the power string.
elements.sent-2411-042: Data from this string and its distributed antennas will also drive the energy resolution obtainable with RNO-G \cite{Aguilar:2021uzt .}
elements.sent-2411-043: The two helper strings provide horizontal baselines and are needed in particular for reconstructing the neutrino arrival direction \cite{Plaisier:2023cxz .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Combined, the three borehole strings form an equilateral triangle with horizontal baselines of $\sim$\SI{35.5 {m equipped with deep sub-arrays of antennas, which provide sufficient azimuthal and zenith reconstruction capabilities \cite{Bouma:2023koi .}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: The near-surface antennas provide a dedicated trigger and provide both additional neutrino effective volume and a tag for air shower backgrounds \cite{DeKockere:2022bto,Pyras:2023crm .}
elements.sent-2411-046: All channels are connected to one instrument box that contains the data acquisition (DAQ) system and provides power and communication (\autoref{sec:instrument ).}
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{minipage}{.4\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth,valign=T]{figures/BigRAID_section_view.jpg} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{.6\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=0.75\textwidth,valign=T]{figures/Radio_Drill_Lifted.jpg} \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth,valign=T]{figures/drill2022.png} \end{minipage} \caption{The BigRAID of RNO-G. Left: Section-view drawing of the drill sonde, which is composed of the anti-torque, drill motor, barrel, and cutters/cutter-head sections. Right (top): Photo of BigRAID testing early in the 2021 summer season, showing the drill sonde supported with the tower structure without the protective tent. At the bottom left in the photo is the `snowblower', which catches and disperses the ice chips collected in the barrel after every drill run. Right (bottom): Performance of the BigRAID drill during the season of 2022 at Summit Station in Greenland, in which four complete RNO-G stations were drilled. The fastest hole was drilled within 13 hours. Further efficiency improvements are planned for future drilling seasons.} \label{fig:drill_rates}
elements.sent-2411-047: The 100-m RNO-G boreholes were drilled using the BigRAID, an electromechanical drill that operates in a similar manner to a conventional ice-coring drill, but instead cuts the entire cylindrical ice-core section into small chips as opposed to preserving a pristine core.
elements.sent-2411-048: These chips are captured in the long drill-barrel section and returned to the surface where they are discarded (in our application), leaving a dry borehole ready for the installation of instrumentation.
elements.sent-2411-049: The BigRAID was developed from the original BAS Rapid Access Isotope Drill (RAID) design, which was optimized for efficient drilling speed and borehole access, instead of core retrieval~\cite{RIX_MULVANEY_HONG_ASHURST_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-050: With a diameter of \SI{28.5 {cm the BigRAID delivers much larger boreholes than the \SI{8.2 {cm diameter RAID.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: The BigRAID sonde is detailed and pictured in \autoref{fig:drill_rates .}
elements.sent-2411-052: The drill-sonde is suspended on a cable, traversing vertically in the hole as controlled by a custom-designed MERMAC winch system from MacArtney.
elements.sent-2411-053: The winch is equipped with a 4-conductor \SI{260 {m cable and has a safe working load of \SI{5 {kN .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: In typical drilling operations, the depth of the hole increases by 1-2~m on any individual run (i.e.\ lowering, drilling, and raising), as the ice chips are collected within the \SI{4 {m long barrel and then brought to the surface.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: The exact drilling depth per run depends on the structure of the ice and how quickly the chips fill the drill barrel and typically decreases with depth.
elements.sent-2411-056: After every drill run, the ice-chippings are discharged from the barrel through a snowblower, which dissipates the chips over the snow surface to avoid concentrated ice deposits at the RNO-G site.
elements.sent-2411-057: The entire BigRAID drilling rig is housed on a 45 ft (\SI{13.7 {m ) long HMW-PE sled for transport, which is towable by a snowcat or similar tracked vehicle.}}
elements.sent-2411-058: The drill-sonde is composed of four sections, from bottom to top: the cutters, the chip-collecting barrel, the motor, and the anti-torque sections.
elements.sent-2411-059: The brushless motor provides up to \SI{220 {Nm continuous torque and its rotator is directly coupled to the barrel (without gearing).}}
elements.sent-2411-060: The barrel is equipped with two cutters at the lower end, which are similar to augers used for ice fishing.
elements.sent-2411-061: These cutters are manufactured from hardened-A2 tool steel that allows for field sharpening, while also remaining sharp for a useful duration (>\SI{100 {m of drilling).}}
elements.sent-2411-062: During drilling, the chips, inside the rotating barrel, are pushed up the flights of a static auger.
elements.sent-2411-063: The motor rotation, direction, and speed can be changed during operations and an encoder allows the cutter position to be accurately set when stopped.
elements.sent-2411-064: At the top of the drill-sonde assembly, the (in-ice) anti-torque section provides engagement with the borehole wall using a set of retractable blades, which counteracts the torque from the driving motor and enables the sonde to drill ice.
elements.sent-2411-065: A separate above-surface anti-torque device is used when starting each hole, during which time the drill-sonde anti-torque section is either above the surface or in soft firn that does not reliably engage.
elements.sent-2411-066: The boreholes are extremely smooth and vertical, showing evidence of only small misalignment from vertical due to the long length of the drill-sonde.
elements.sent-2411-067: Inclinometers were added to the sonde in 2024 to obtain precise measurements in future drilling campaigns.
elements.sent-2411-068: During the 2022 season, the BigRAID and its operators delivered twelve 100~m deep instrumentation holes during an 8-week drilling season at Summit Camp, which includes constructing and packing up the drill setup, as shown with the per-hole completion rates in \autoref{fig:drill_rates .}
elements.sent-2411-069: The 2022 drilling effort also encountered two cases of a stuck drill sonde, one at shallow depth ($\sim$4m) and the second deep ($\sim$86m).
elements.sent-2411-070: The sonde was successfully retrieved in both situations and modest redesign efforts are ongoing to mitigate stuck-drill issues going forward.
elements.sent-2411-071: Efforts to automate the steering and drilling process are also ongoing, to streamline the number of holes that can be drilled in the upcoming field seasons.

elements.sent-2411-072: In this section, we will describe the RNO-G instrumental design in detail.
elements.sent-2411-073: The antennas are the primary sensors for the RNO-G experiment, receiving electric fields generated by neutrino interactions, the background thermal noise from the ice, anthropogenic transmitters on the ice surface, and other radio sources.
elements.sent-2411-074: Identifiable sources are reconstructed by using time-domain interferometry across the receiving antennas within the station, through the measurement of the radio-wave polarization, and through other properties such as the impulsivity and frequency content of the signal.
elements.sent-2411-075: The antennas are characterized by a complex impedance and gain response.
elements.sent-2411-076: We distinguish between `in-air' and `in-ice' as these attributes are dependent on the dielectric environment in which the antennas are installed.
elements.sent-2411-077: The in-air simulations allow a more straightforward verification of antenna performance in the lab, while the in-ice modeling describes the actual performance within the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-078: The voltage standing wave ratio (VSWR) describes the impedance match between the output of the antenna and input of the front-end amplifier via the expression \begin{equation}\mathrm{VSWR} = \frac{1+ \left| \rho \right|}{1 - \left| \rho \right|}\end{equation} where $\rho$ is the frequency-dependent complex voltage reflection coefficient.
elements.sent-2411-079: The front-end amplifier units are designed to present a constant 50~$\Omega$ real-valued impedance across the RNO-G signal band (see \autoref{sec:rf_chain ), such that the frequency dependence of the VSWR is largely determined by the antenna impedance.}
elements.sent-2411-080: To maximize the transfer of signal power to the RF signal chain, the RNO-G antennas are designed to match this 50$\Omega$ impedance as best as possible.
elements.sent-2411-081: The antenna gain describes the directive amplitude response, which corresponds to the effective power-collecting area of the antenna.
elements.sent-2411-082: As RNO-G directly measures the voltage waveforms, the antenna vector effective length (VEL) best characterizes the antenna gain response.
elements.sent-2411-083: The VEL, defined along the antenna E-plane axis, relates the incident electric field vector to the open-circuit output voltage $V_{OC}$ of the antenna and is given by the expression \begin{equation}V_{OC} = \overrightarrow{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{VEL}}\end{equation} For a perfectly matched antenna and receiver system, the voltage actually measured is $V = V_{OC /2$.}
elements.sent-2411-084: More generally, we characterize a realized VEL (RVEL) that includes the effects of impedance mismatches between the antenna and receiver as described by the VSWR.
elements.sent-2411-085: The RVEL corresponds to the realized gain pattern of the antenna.
elements.sent-2411-086: The RNO-G station employs three different antenna types.
elements.sent-2411-087: A commercial log-periodic dipole array (LPDA) antenna is used near the surface, which provides a relatively high-gain directional response.
elements.sent-2411-088: For the deep receivers, a set of custom cylindrically-symmetric antennas were designed with the constraints imposed by the borehole-installation requirement.
elements.sent-2411-089: Of the borehole antennas, one is a vertically-polarized antenna (Vpol) that is sensitive to the vertical component of the electric field, and the other is a horizontally-polarized antenna (Hpol) that inductively couples to a magnetic field along the antenna axis, providing a measure of horizontal electric fields.
elements.sent-2411-090: The antennas were designed and characterized with a finite-difference time-domain antenna simulation software (XFdtd) and their as-built responses have been measured in the lab.
elements.sent-2411-091: In-ice measurements have also been performed, but will be presented in a detailed follow-on calibration paper.
elements.sent-2411-092: The borehole antennas include water-jet cut nylon endcaps that have a diameter of 8 and 9.5 inches (20.3 and 24.1 cm) for the Vpols and Hpols, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-093: The endcaps provide a housing for the downhole front-end amplifiers and fiber transceivers, protection for fiber and cable routing, and serves as a minimal `spacer' between the antenna and the ice.
elements.sent-2411-094: The spacer functionality aids in centralizing the antennas within the borehole, which has been shown in simulations to reduce systematic uncertainties in the antenna response due to potential offsets in the borehole~\cite{RNO-G:2021upe .}
elements.sent-2411-095: Crucially, the endcaps also prevent the metal antenna elements from contacting the borehole wall, which was found to induce a severe azimuthal asymmetry in the antenna response in detailed simulations.
elements.sent-2411-096: The borehole antenna units span \SI{90 {cm in vertical length when the frame, endcaps, and front-end amplifier enclosures are fully installed.}}
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth, trim={0 2.5cm 0 0cm},clip]{figures/vpol_fig_instrument_paper.pdf} \caption{The RNO-G vertically-polarized (Vpol) borehole antenna. Bottom left: CAD model of the Vpol and the assembled Vpol antenna in the lab, including the deployment frame and rigging rope. Top left: Measured in-air VSWR of all assembled and shipped RNO-G Vpols (QTY $\sim$250, shown in light green) compared to XFdtd simulations in-air (red) and deep ice (black). Top right: Theta (co-polarized) component of the realized vector effective length measured in-air (solid line) as compared to XFdtd simulations in-air (dashed) and deep ice (dotted), shown at two different zenith angles. Bottom right: Realized gain pattern of the Vpol at representative frequencies, simulated in the deep ice borehole.} \label{fig:vpol_display}
elements.sent-2411-097: The Vpol antennas, shown in \autoref{fig:vpol_display , are a fat-dipole antenna design with a symmetric feedpoint between two 12.7~cm diameter cylindrical elements for a total length of 60~cm~\cite{RNO-G:2021upe .}}
elements.sent-2411-098: The wide diameter of the elements provides the broad bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-099: The feed transition design, following from the RICE~\cite{Kravchenko:2011im and GNO dipole~\cite{Avva:2016ggs antennas, uses a conical section screwed into the sides of the aluminum walls; the opening-angle of which was tuned to optimize the VSWR.}}
elements.sent-2411-100: The conical transition section for the RNO-G dipole is made from a set of 8 machined spokes to reduce the overall weight and cost.
elements.sent-2411-101: A 50~$\Omega$ SMA connector sits at the feedpoint and effectively provides the balanced-to-unbalanced conversion (balun) to the coaxial cable.
elements.sent-2411-102: A 12" RG316 double-shielded coaxial cable connects the antenna feed to the low-noise amplifier unit housed just above the antenna.
elements.sent-2411-103: The Vpols have a 2:1 VSWR in-ice bandwidth of $\sim$\SI{140 -\SI{600 {MHz as shown in \autoref{fig:vpol_display .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-104: Before shipping to the field, each fabricated antenna is measured in the lab and shows excellent agreement to the in-air simulated VSWR.
elements.sent-2411-105: The zenith-angle response pattern has a clear primary boresight beam until $\sim$\SI{550 {MHz , above which the response starts to go multimodal.}}
elements.sent-2411-106: The simulated and measured RVELs are also shown in \autoref{fig:vpol_display , with the measurement done in an anechoic chamber.}
elements.sent-2411-107: There is good agreement between the measured and simulated in-air RVEL at the 10$\%$ level.
elements.sent-2411-108: Once embedded in the deep ice (n=1.8) borehole, the RVEL response curve shifts down by 30-40~MHz, which roughly tracks the same shift given by the simulated in-ice VSWR.
elements.sent-2411-109: Identical Vpols are also embedded in the station as transmitting antennas used for the calibration pulsers, which are installed on the two helper strings as well as near the surface.
elements.sent-2411-110: These are equipped with different front-end analog amplifier units that are designed for pulsed-radio transmission instead of reception.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth, trim={0 2.0cm 0 1.2cm},clip]{figures/hpol_fig_instrument_paper.pdf} \caption{The RNO-G horizontally-polarized (Hpol) borehole antenna. Bottom left: CAD model of the Hpol and the assembled Hpol antenna in the lab, including the deployment frame and rigging rope. Top left: Measured in-air VSWR of all assembled and shipped RNO-G Hpols (QTY $\sim$70, shown in light green) compared to XFdtd simulations in-air (red) and deep ice (black). Top right: Phi (co-polarized) component of the realized vector effective length from XFdtd simulations in-air (dashed) and deep ice (dotted), shown at two different zenith angles, including boresight (90$^\circ$) compared to a lab measurement. Bottom right: Realized gain pattern of the Hpol at representative frequencies, simulated in the deep ice borehole.} \label{fig:hpol_XFDTD}
elements.sent-2411-111: The Hpol antennas, shown in \autoref{fig:hpol_XFDTD , are a quad-slotted cylinder antenna design with a passive matching and feed network.}
elements.sent-2411-112: Slotted cylinder antennas offer a reasonably high gain-bandwidth solution for measuring the toroidal electric field component, given practical limitations of the borehole installations.
elements.sent-2411-113: The slotted antennas operate via Booker's extension of Babinet's principle to radiate/receive perpendicular to the vertical slots in the cylinder~\cite{RNO-G:2023xyg .}
elements.sent-2411-114: The wider BigRAID \SI{28.5 {cm boreholes allow for optimizing the quad-slot design: for a single-slotted cylinder, the antenna diameter increases the low-end bandwidth.}}
elements.sent-2411-115: However, to maintain cylindrical symmetry in the beam response, four slots were implemented that get combined into a single feed-point.
elements.sent-2411-116: The slot multiplicity again reduces the low-end bandwidth due to decreasing the shunt inductance of the cylinder walls.
elements.sent-2411-117: The Hpol quad-slot design was informed by ARA, which solved this low-frequency response issue by loading the inside of the cylindrical antenna with a large amount of ferrite material~\cite{Allison:2011wk .}
elements.sent-2411-118: This improved the low-frequency response (down to $\sim$150~MHz), but proved difficult to accurately model in simulations and made for a rather heavy antenna unit.
elements.sent-2411-119: The RNO-G quad-slot design did not consider adding ferrites, but rather used a passive (reactive) matching network, feeding each slot, to get the best performance from the antenna.
elements.sent-2411-120: The matching network is formed using a hub-and-spoke printed circuit board design, with four spoke boards feeding each balanced slot with the matching network, and connected at the hub in parallel.
elements.sent-2411-121: The hub incorporates an SMA connector in the center that serves as the balun.
elements.sent-2411-122: Similar to the Vpols, a 12" RG316 double-shielded coaxial cable connects the SMA balun to the low-noise amplifier unit housed just above the antenna.
elements.sent-2411-123: The RNO-G quad-slotted cylinder is 60 cm long, matching the length of the Vpol antennas to get a similar gain-response pattern, and has an outer diameter of 20.3~cm (8").
elements.sent-2411-124: As with the Vpol antennas, each Hpol is measured in the lab before shipping to the field, and shows excellent agreement to the in-air simulated VSWR as shown in \autoref{fig:hpol_XFDTD .}
elements.sent-2411-125: The Hpols have a 3:1 VSWR in-ice bandwidth of \SI{300 -\SI{850 {MHz .}}}
elements.sent-2411-126: Overall the peak response for the Hpol is $\sim$2-3~dB lower than the Vpol, and sits predominately in a higher frequency band though there is considerable overlap in the two antenna bandwidths by design.
elements.sent-2411-127: The RNO-G experiment does not currently implement a trigger using the Hpols, but they are important for event reconstruction in the deep sub-array of the station.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth, trim={0 1.5cm 0 1.5cm},clip]{figures/lpda_fig_instrument_paper.pdf} \caption{ The Create CLP-5130-2N LPDA used for the RNO-G near-surface antennas. Bottom left: Photo of the assembled LPDA in the lab. Top left: Measured in-air VSWR for a single LPDA (green dashed) compared to an in-air WIPL-D simulation (red). elements.sent-2411-128: The LPDAs are commercially-procured antennas, manufactured by Create with model number CLP-5130-2N and are the same antennas as used in the ARIANNA experiment where they were characterized in detail~\cite{Barwick:2014boa,Barwick:2016mxm .}
elements.sent-2411-129: These antennas are shipped disassembled, for compact packing, and assembled in the field.
elements.sent-2411-130: The LPDAs are installed in trenches just below the snow surface in three separate rows that are centered on each borehole radial axis.
elements.sent-2411-131: Each row of LPDAs consists of three LPDAs, two that are canted downward at approximately a 60$^{\circ}$ angle and one that is directed upward (see top left of \autoref{fig:sys_diagram ).}
elements.sent-2411-132: The upward pointing LPDAs are intended to be used as a cosmic ray and surface background veto, while the two downward pointing antennas add a secondary contribution to the overall neutrino effective volume of the detector.
elements.sent-2411-133: As they are buried just below the surface, they are not limited by the geometric constraints of the boreholes and thus can be much larger than the borehole antennas.
elements.sent-2411-134: This affords a higher gain and lower turn-on frequency than the borehole antennas, as can be seen in the REVL shown in~\autoref{fig:LPDA_insitu_s11 .}
elements.sent-2411-135: These LPDAs, as an established broadband dipole-array design, also have an extremely broad bandwidth from \SI{100 - \SI{1000 {MHz with VSWR better than 2:1 over that range.}}}
elements.sent-2411-136: The RF system was designed to balance the small power budget with the low-noise requirements, and to fit within small and high-density form factors.
elements.sent-2411-137: With these instrumental constraints, the signal chains are fully custom as there were no commercially available options meeting these specifications.
elements.sent-2411-138: The deep borehole receiving antennas are equipped with a custom RF-over-fiber (RFoF) implementation for long-distance signal transport, which is required in order to maintain a $\sim$flat gain profile and a manageable borehole-string design.
elements.sent-2411-139: The In-ice Gain with Low-noise Unit (IGLU) is the front-end RF module that is installed directly above each borehole antenna, and includes a 50~$\Omega$-matched low-noise amplifier chip (Infineon BGB741l7ESD) and custom amplitude-modulated RFoF transmitter circuit.
elements.sent-2411-140: The IGLU draws a total of 43~mA on a 3.3V input rail, meeting the ultra-low power requirement of a fraction of a Watt per channel.
elements.sent-2411-141: The Downhole Receiver and Amplifier Board (DRAB) is a quad-channel board that receives the optical signal from each IGLU, re-converts to an electrical signal, and further filters and amplifies the signal before sending it to the data acquisition boards.
elements.sent-2411-142: The DRAB is installed within the main instrument enclosure at the surface.
elements.sent-2411-143: A block diagram of the RNO-G RFoF systems, including the downhole calibration pulser system, is shown in \autoref{fig:dh_rf_diagram .}
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.97\textwidth]{figures/rno-g-rf-diagram.jpg} \caption{Block diagram of the downhole receiver and calibration-transmit RFoF systems. On the left are the in-ice IGLU and IGLU-cal units, which interface to the DRAB receivers and Calibration Transmitter board in the surface instrument box over 4- or 6-channel fiber bundles.} \label{fig:dh_rf_diagram}
elements.sent-2411-144: The surface antenna receivers, called the SURFACE boards, are custom highly-integrated circuit boards that connect directly to the LPDA antennas via 10~m LMR400 coaxial cables.
elements.sent-2411-145: The five-channel SURFACE boards have three amplification and bandpass filtering stages per channel, using the same first-stage LNA chip as the IGLU, and are housed in a tight-fitting custom enclosure lined with RF-absorbing material to eliminate cross-talk.
elements.sent-2411-146: Unlike the IGLUs, the SURFACE receivers include an input RF power-limiter on each channel to prevent damage to the signal chain from large radio signals that could be generated in close proximity to the near-surface LPDA antennas.
elements.sent-2411-147: Both the surface and downhole signal chains are described in further detail in \cite{RNO-G:2023fkv .}
elements.sent-2411-148: The total 24-channel RF system power draw for the RNO-G instrument is just under 6 W.
elements.sent-2411-149: The additive noise temperature from the RF system was required to be sub-dominant to the ice temperature of $\sim$240~K, which is the primary contributor to the antenna temperature of both the borehole-installed Vpol/Hpols and downward-pointed surface antennas.
elements.sent-2411-150: This performance metric has been confirmed in the fielded stations (see \autoref{sec:ext_radio_sig ) and has also been measured in the lab to be below 140~K for both chains across the full receiving bandwidth as shown in \autoref{fig:chain_noise .}}
elements.sent-2411-151: For the deep antennas, the noise temperature of the IGLU-DRAB chain is below 80~K in the 100-400~MHz bandwidth range that primarily contributes to the neutrino trigger.
elements.sent-2411-152: The gain profile of the RF system has three major requirements: 1) bring the baseline antenna voltage-output signal to a measurable level of at least 5-10~mV$_{\mathrm{rms }$, 2) preserve a linear response for signals with amplitudes at or greater than $30\times$ above the thermal noise level, and 3) sufficiently reject out-of-band noise, such as the wireless communications signals that sit just above 850~MHz.}
elements.sent-2411-153: The thermal noise power received by a perfectly impedance-matched antenna viewing a constant ice temperature is given by \begin{math k_{B T \Delta f \end{math .}}}
elements.sent-2411-154: Over a 100-700~MHz bandwidth with an ice temperature of 240K, this is equivalent to a baseline thermal noise power of $\sim$2~pW presented to the input of the RF signal chain.
elements.sent-2411-155: This corresponds to an input-referred voltage level of $\sim$10~$\mu$$V_{\mathrm{rms }$ in a 50~$\Omega$ system and sets a minimum gain requirement of approximately 1000$\times$ in voltage amplitude, or 60~dB.}
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[trim={2.5cm 1.5cm 2.0cm 2.4cm},clip,width=0.8\textwidth]{figures/icrc_noise_temp.pdf} \caption{Noise temperature of the borehole (IGLU-DRAB) and surface antenna signal-amplifier chains, at the relevant physical front-end temperatures, as measured in the lab using a climate-controlled chamber. } \label{fig:chain_noise}
elements.sent-2411-156: The gain and input return loss (RL) responses of both the downhole and surface signal chains are shown in \autoref{fig:chain_forward_gain .}
elements.sent-2411-157: Both chains have a gain in the range of 61-63~dB in-band and maintain a relatively flat frequency profile.
elements.sent-2411-158: Also, both chains are well matched to 50~$\Omega$ input over the RNO-G signal bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-159: The downhole chain has a RL that exceeds 10~dB (2:1 VSWR) from 110-720~MHz, and the surface chain meets the same performance over a 70-720~MHz bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-160: A lower frequency cut-off was designed for the surface chain to accommodate the wider bandwidth of LPDA antennas.
elements.sent-2411-161: The full magnitude and phase responses for all RF signal-chain components are saved into a database as complex S-parameters for use in later calibration and data analysis efforts.
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{figures/rnog_paper_rf_sparams.pdf} \caption{Representative gain and input return loss (RL) performance of the RNO-G RF signal chains. Left: The forward gain (|S21|) of the two signal chains, meeting the >60~dB specification. The bandwidth profile of the two chains are fairly closely matched; the surface profile is smoother without the fiber transceiver components. Right: The S11 magnitude response, which is a measure of the RL. Both chains are well matched to 50S$\Omega$ given by the -10~dB target specification: the downhole and surface chains meet 10~dB RL specifications from 110-720~MHz and 70-720~MHz, respectively.} \label{fig:chain_forward_gain}
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=0.99\textwidth]{figures/deep.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.99\textwidth]{figures/shallow.pdf} \caption{In-situ calibration pulser waveforms from station 23. Top: Pulse transmitted from an in-situ deep Vpol pulser on a helper string and received by the lowest six antennas of the power string. The waveforms of the lowest four Vpols (the phased array) and two Hpols are shown. A small amount of cross-polarization response is visible in the Hpol channels. This transmitting antenna is installed equidistant between two of the three LPDA trenches, and the corresponding received pulse amplitude response tracks the antenna gain pattern as expected from their orientation: CH14/CH15 are installed in separate trenches and both pointed towards the transmitter, CH13/CH16 are the upward-pointing LPDAs in each trench, and CH12/17 are pointed away from the pulser. } \label{figure:calpulser}
elements.sent-2411-162: The RNO-G instrument incorporates a pair of deep calibration pulsers on the helper strings to test the in-situ performance of the receiver channels.
elements.sent-2411-163: These are essentially implemented as the optical reverse path to the IGLU-DRAB receivers, as shown in \autoref{fig:dh_rf_diagram .}
elements.sent-2411-164: Each borehole transmitting antenna is installed with an IGLU-Cal module that receives the optical pulser signal over fiber, re-converts to an electrical signal, and applies a last-stage signal amplification before sending to the antenna feed.
elements.sent-2411-165: The IGLU, used for the receiving antennas, and the IGLU-Cal are housed in identical RF enclosures to maintain a uniform module design for the borehole antennas.
elements.sent-2411-166: All of the installed station transmitters use Vpol antennas.
elements.sent-2411-167: The Calibration Transmit Board generates the RF signals for the calibration pulsers, and includes a fast impulse generator and a pair of continuous sine-wave (CW) frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-168: The selected signal type can be attenuated over a 31.25~dB range via remote digital control.
elements.sent-2411-169: Additionally, there are four selectable RF outputs from the Calibration Transmit Board: two RFoF transmitters, which are routed to the IGLU-Cal modules on the helper strings, and two coaxial outputs.
elements.sent-2411-170: One of the coaxial outputs is sent to a near-surface transmitting Vpol antenna over standard LMR240 or LMR400 cable, while the other is typically only used in lab testing.
elements.sent-2411-171: Only one output from the Calibration Transmit Board can be active at a given time.
elements.sent-2411-172: The Calibration Transmit Board is managed by the RNO-G station controller board (\autoref{sec:control ) and can be disabled when not in use to conserve power resources.}
elements.sent-2411-173: The near-surface calibration transmit antenna is typically dug-in by hand, at most 2~m deep.
elements.sent-2411-174: At two RNO-G-7 stations (21 and 23), this pulser antenna was installed in a deeper augered borehole, between 10 and 20~m depth.
elements.sent-2411-175: Such depths allow more favorable transmit-receive angles between the LPDAs and the upper Vpol antennas on the power string, and enable monitoring of the near-surface firn properties~\cite{Anker:2019zcx .}
elements.sent-2411-176: It is envisioned to install near-surface pulsers at such depths for future RNO-G stations.
elements.sent-2411-177: The in-situ calibration system is used to validate the station operation immediately after installation and over the lifetime of the station.
elements.sent-2411-178: The resulting calibration dataset can be used to determine the relative positions of the antennas and, to some level, the index of refraction model that describes the ice as a function of depth.
elements.sent-2411-179: The pulsers are also used to validate the trigger design (\autoref{sec:trigger ), and to measure the in-situ time-domain system response (\autoref{sec:tdomain ).}}
elements.sent-2411-180: Example calibration pulser event waveforms are shown in \autoref{figure:calpulser , showing the deep antennas on the power string as received from the helper string pulser and a selection of the shallow LPDA antennas as received from the near-surface pulser.}
elements.sent-2411-181: The design of the borehole antenna strings was carefully considered in order to reduce component count, ease the deployment process, and to maintain uniform antenna responses.
elements.sent-2411-182: This is largely accomplished through the design of the constituent borehole antenna `units' (\autoref{sec:antennas ), which are compact pre-assembled and tested modules that integrate the antenna, mechanical frame, and front-end IGLU (or IGLU-Cal) RF enclosure.}
elements.sent-2411-183: Each antenna unit has a simplex fiber connection (FC/APC) for the RFoF signal and a coaxial connection (N-type) for power.
elements.sent-2411-184: A LMR240 through-power cable is included in the pre-assembled antenna unit, which passes power from the IGLU, installed above the antenna, down through the internal unit exposing a bottom-side connector that is available to attach to either the next antenna or a run of LMR400 cable.
elements.sent-2411-185: Therefore, each antenna unit has the same conductive through-cabling power element and the same length of feeding coaxial cable, which maintains a common impulse-response for the Vpols and Hpols, separately.
elements.sent-2411-186: The fiber-optic signal cables, which are routed up the array on the outside of the antennas, are of negligible influence on the antenna response.
elements.sent-2411-187: This borehole string design concept is inherited from the ARA phased array~\cite{Allison:2018ynt , which demonstrated well-matched impulse responses among many antennas in a compact string.}

Middle: The uppermost Vpol antenna on the string, which is ultimately installed at a depth of 40~m as shown in \autoref{fig:array_and_station_layout}. The staggered-length fibers are visible as is the final power connection to the string via a LMR400 cable with a clamp-on ferrite. Right: The string as it is lowered into place, with the last string centralizer visible that sits above the highest upper Vpol antenna. The power string uses three long-run cables, two ruggedized multi-channel fiber cables and a single power cable.} \label{fig:powerstring}
elements.sent-2411-188: The RNO-G borehole string installation process is shown at a few intermediate stages in \autoref{fig:powerstring .}
elements.sent-2411-189: These photos show the power string installation, which includes both the compact deep set of 6-antenna (4 Vpols + 2 Hpols) deployed with 1~m center-to-center spacing and the upper Vpol antennas with much longer baselines of 20~m.
elements.sent-2411-190: Also visible is the separate string-centralizer device, which is an extremely simple part made from flexible HDPE that further minimizes antenna offsets within the borehole.
elements.sent-2411-191: These are installed directly above the deep set of antennas on each string, and also above each upper Vpol in the power string.
elements.sent-2411-192: The depths of all components are recorded using an open-reel tape measure that is spooled out and installed with the string.
elements.sent-2411-193: Rugged single-mode fiber cables are used for the RFoF signals.
elements.sent-2411-194: The simplex fiber from each antenna unit is connected to a bulk multi-channel fiber bundle that is routed to the instrument box on the surface.
elements.sent-2411-195: These cables are included in the RF signal chain calibrations to reflect the added group delay from the cable length.
elements.sent-2411-196: A thermal coefficient of delay (TCD) for these fibers has been measured at $\mathcal{O}$(\qty{100 {ps \: K^{-1 \: km^{-1 ), which is a significant, but constant delay adjustment from the room temperature lab measurements to the fielded installation and will be discussed in a follow-on detailed calibration paper.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-197: All fiber cables are rated to -55$^{\circ}$C.
elements.sent-2411-198: When operating in the low-power winter-mode (\autoref{sec:power_stuff ), the downhole IGLUs are powered off until sufficient power becomes available to return to science operations.}
elements.sent-2411-199: Each borehole string has a dedicated power switch on the station controller board and the current draw can be independently monitored.
elements.sent-2411-200: For RNO-G-7, the strings have been powered down for 2+ winters, all returning to nominal condition without a failure.
elements.sent-2411-201: The RNO-G instrument records time snap-shots of full-bandwidth voltage waveforms on all 24 receiver channels at a sampling rate between 2.4 and 3.2~Gigasamples-per-second~(GSa/s) with a sample resolution of 12-bits.
elements.sent-2411-202: Events are recorded based on a set of hardware-level triggers, taken typically at an overall per-station rate of $\sim$1~Hz.
elements.sent-2411-203: There is a back-end station controller board, based on a low-power single-board computer (SBC), that controls the data acquisition (DAQ) and trigger systems, and saves the waveform data to a local micro-SD card.
elements.sent-2411-204: These DAQ hardware systems and associated embedded software are described below.
elements.sent-2411-205: Each channel is equipped with a LAB4D~\cite{Roberts:2018xyf , a custom ASIC switched capacitor array with a sampling rate of up to $\qty{3.2 {GSa/s}$ and an analog memory of 4096 samples, addressable in 128-sample blocks, which allows for the capability of on-chip multi-event buffering to reduce readout-induced dead-time.}}
elements.sent-2411-206: For the purposes of RNO-G, the LAB4D operates with two buffers of 2048~samples each, providing a $\qty{640 {ns}$ recording window per event when operating at 3.2~GSa/s.}
elements.sent-2411-207: These two analog buffers are utilized in a back-and-forth manner for each subsequent recorded event.
elements.sent-2411-208: The LAB4D has an on-chip ramp-compare ADC that provides digital data with 12-bit resolution and this conversion takes $\sim$10~$\mu$s per event.
elements.sent-2411-209: The digital conversion and readout of the LAB4D data is only performed when the system is triggered.
elements.sent-2411-210: The Radiant Artix-7 FPGA controls most of the functionality on the board, including managing the control and readout of the 24~LAB4D chips.
elements.sent-2411-211: Due to pin-count limitations on the FPGA, some of the board control lines are handled by a pair of lower-level complex programmable logic device devices (CPLD).
elements.sent-2411-212: The waveform data are sent to the station controller board over a dedicated serial link, while slow control is handled by a separate on-board microcontroller for configuring the board.
elements.sent-2411-213: The Radiant has a set of on-board signal sources that enable in situ calibration of the LAB4D ASICs without a need for external signal inputs.
elements.sent-2411-214: There is an on-board sine-wave generator circuit that uses a direct-digital synthesizer chip with a configurable output filter bank, which suppresses the higher harmonics.
elements.sent-2411-215: These sine waves are distributed to the LAB4Ds in groups of 8 channels via an RF switch on each input channel and are used to calibrate the LAB4D time-base.
elements.sent-2411-216: Each channel also has a programmable DC-offset voltage, which is common for all channels, to set the bias voltage at the input of the LAB4D, which can record signals between 0.1 and $\sim$2.0~V.
elements.sent-2411-217: The bias level can be scanned (`bias scans') over this range to calibrate the ADC transfer function for each sample within the LAB4D chips.
elements.sent-2411-218: For normal data acquisition, this bias voltage is set at a static level that optimizes the linear range of the ADC.
elements.sent-2411-219: These board-level calibrations are run at regular intervals within the instrument to keep the Radiant response up-to-date in the field.
elements.sent-2411-220: The Radiant version 2 board (v2) was developed for the initial 2021 season and is used in the RNO-G-7 array described in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-221: During 2023, a new revision 3 board (v3) was designed, fabricated, and deployed in the 2024 field season to partially upgrade the RNO-G-7 array.
elements.sent-2411-222: The Radiant v3 enhances the stability and cold operation of the board, added LAB4D on-board calibration options, and improved sensitivity of the on-board Radiant trigger.
elements.sent-2411-223: This paper will focus on the RNO-G station performance with the Radiant v2, and the upgraded stations will be discussed in a future publication.
elements.sent-2411-224: The FLexible Octal WavEform Recorder (Flower) board is the other component of the DAQ and trigger system.
elements.sent-2411-225: For RNO-G, this board is hardware-wired with four input channels such that RF signals are captured using two 8-bit streaming digitizers (HMCAD1511) at a sampling rate of \SI{472 {MSa/s , slightly below the original design target of \SI{500 {MSa/s due to an issue with the ADC-clock generating chip.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-226: The digitized samples are streamed to a Cyclone~V~FPGA where they are deserialized at a parallel clock rate of 118~MHz, which drives the core logic.
elements.sent-2411-227: The Flower can be used to save the waveforms on these four channels, with a record length of up to 20~$\mu$s/channel, but its primary function is to provide the deep neutrino trigger for the RNO-G instrument.
elements.sent-2411-228: The signals for the four lower Vpols on the power string (`phased-array' as denoted in \autoref{fig:array_and_station_layout ) are sent through an RF power splitter, low-pass filtered at $\sim$\SI{240 {MHz to prevent aliasing from the lower sampling rate, and sent to the Flower where they are digitized and processed through custom trigger firmware.}}}
elements.sent-2411-229: Both the Radiant and Flower have a common link to the RNO-G station controller (\autoref{sec:control ).}
elements.sent-2411-230: In addition to sending waveform data, these links also allow for the application FPGA firmware on each board to be remotely updated, although a lab-verified and programmed base firwmare image is also maintained on the boards.
elements.sent-2411-231: A common 10~MHz reference clock and global timing reference are distributed to each board, allowing the RNO-G instrument to be fully synchronized.
elements.sent-2411-232: During science operations of the RNO-G instrument, the Radiant draws between 13-14~W and the Flower draws 3~W, with baseline firmware, up to 5~W with more complicated trigger algorithms.
elements.paragraph-2411-001: Voltage and Timing Calibration of the Radiant
elements.sent-2411-233: The LAB4D requires amplitude and time-base calibrations, which are common for such custom waveform sampling ASICs.
elements.sent-2411-234: With the internal on-chip ADC, each of the 4096 analog samples has a voltage-to-adu (adu = ADC count) transfer function.
elements.sent-2411-235: The LAB4D time-base also is calibrated, primarily through tuning configurable delays in the on-chip timebase generator that drives the 128-cell primary sampling array.
elements.sent-2411-236: Individual sample delays along the 128-sample SCA, subject to CMOS process variations that induce sample-to-sample timing errors, each have a delay element that can be tuned to reduce this error.
elements.sent-2411-237: Separately, the `wraparound' time interval, which is the seam that closes the primary sampling delay-locked-loop from \verb|sample-128| back to \verb|sample-1|, has an independent delay control block.
elements.sent-2411-238: The LAB4D calibration process is described in detail in~\cite{Roberts:2018xyf .}

On Radiant start-up, the wrap-around time-step, which is the timing interval from the last primary sample cell back to the first sample, needs to be set to the correct value to establish a uniform timebase. These calibration data were obtained with the on-board signal generator providing a \SI{510}{MHz} sine wave. } \label{fig:RADIANT_wave}

elements.sent-2411-250: A number of trigger options are built into an RNO-G station, including the Flower trigger used for neutrino detection by deep antennas, the Radiant trigger used for surface antennas, the GPS PPS trigger to sync to external calibration sources, and the software trigger to monitor background noise levels.
elements.sent-2411-251: All of these triggers can be enabled simultaneously while taking data, and the target rates for each type can be set in a configuration file.
elements.sent-2411-252: An overview of the RF-specific trigger architecture is shown in \autoref{fig:trigger_block , which describes the Flower trigger and Radiant trigger interfaces, comprising the Flower and Radiant boards, which will be described in the following.}
elements.figure-2411-015: \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{figures/rno-g-trig-diagram.jpg} \caption{RF trigger diagram. The three built-in self-trigger paths are shown, which form the flexible station trigger using streaming digital data on the 4-channel Flower and the two configurable Radiant coincidence triggers. This represents the Radiant v2 design that was used until the 2024 season; the 10~dB couplers in the Radiant trigger path were swapped out for 3~dB splitters in the Radiant v3.} \label{fig:trigger_block}
elements.sent-2411-253: A small DC bias ($\sim$100~$\mu$A) is applied to the diode circuit to stabilize the response over temperature~\cite{AN1090 .}
elements.sent-2411-254: The diode output is restored and conditioned using $\sim$40~dB of amplification and low-pass filters, respectively, such that the bandwidth of the signal-envelope sent to the discriminator (e.g. the video bandwidth) is $\sim$\SI{0 -\SI{30 {MHz , ensuring that the primary RF signal band is sufficiently rejected.}}}
elements.sent-2411-255: Each Radiant channel is equipped with this diode detector circuit, which is coupled off the mainline LAB4D signal path with a -10~dB coupler.
elements.sent-2411-256: This was adjusted to a -3~dB splitter in the Radiant v3 to increase the signal power in the trigger path.
elements.sent-2411-257: The nine surface channels have an added 200~MHz low-pass filter (weak roll-off, 2nd order) in the trigger path, after the coupler and before the diode, which improves the trigger-level signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) for cosmic-ray air showers as received by the LPDAs, which have stronger low-frequency signals~\cite{masterthesis-pyras .}
elements.sent-2411-258: The output of each diode detector is fed to one of the differential inputs of a LVDS (Low-Voltage Differential Signaling) input of the Radiant FPGA.
elements.sent-2411-259: The opposite input is connected to a voltage threshold that is generated from an digital-to-analog (DAC) made from an integrated pulse-width modulation circuit.
elements.sent-2411-260: This architecture effectively employs the LVDS input block of the FPGA as a threshold-discriminator circuit and is easily scaled to high-channel count density with low power, as demonstrated in the ANITA experiment~\cite{Varner:2006ec .}
elements.sent-2411-261: The design goal was for each diode trigger to be thermal-noise riding with adjusted thresholds to provide a stable coincident trigger rate $\ll$\qty{1 {Hz .}}
elements.sent-2411-262: However, the observed reduced sensitivity of the Radiant v2 diode trigger required these to be operated typically with a noise-avoiding threshold well above the thermal noise, where the threshold was dynamically adjusted to keep the single-channel rate well under approximately 10 Hz.
elements.sent-2411-263: Within the FPGA, the digital output from each discriminator is sent to two coincidence trigger blocks, where channels can be masked and arbitrary trigger delays can be applied to form the Radiant trigger, as shown in~\autoref{fig:trigger_block .}
elements.sent-2411-264: In science operations, the RNO-G instrument employs the first Radiant coincidence trigger on the three upward-facing LPDA antennas as an air shower veto.
elements.sent-2411-265: This trigger is typically configured with a 2-of-3 coincidence that must be fulfilled within \qty{60 {ns .}}
elements.sent-2411-266: The second Radiant coincidence trigger utilizes the six downward-canted LPDA antennas and must typically meet a 2-of-6 criterion.
elements.sent-2411-267: This reduces triggers due to random fluctuations, since the assumed plane wave generated by a particle cascade is expected to be measured by at least two antennas.
elements.sent-2411-268: These coincidence triggers are sometimes applied, via remote configuration, to the deep Hpol antennas, for example, when doing calibration pulsing.
elements.sent-2411-269: Lab measurements show that the efficiency of the trigger circuit depends on the pulse shape, the temperature and the diode bias.
elements.sent-2411-270: A trigger efficiency of 6.2~SNR was achieved in the field (v2) using the surface pulser, though a large threshold variance is observed across channels.
elements.sent-2411-271: Lab testing on the Radiant v3 demonstrates improved performance of the diode trigger circuit, which is expected to be able to operate stably at much higher single-channel rates.
elements.sent-2411-272: The updated design was tested on air-shower-like signals in the lab, which resulted in a trigger efficiency of 4.1~SNR and a much more stable variance between channels.
elements.sent-2411-273: These four signals are split off from the main Radiant path using broadband 3dB splitters, which are uniquely applied to these channels.
elements.sent-2411-274: The Flower trigger operates on a \SI{100 -\SI{240 {MHz bandwidth, which was found to improve the trigger-level SNR for a wide range of Askaryan off-cone view angles, as this corresponds to the frequency range (<\SI{300 {MHz ) where in-ice Vpol RVEL provides maximum voltage response~(\autoref{fig:vpol_display ).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-275: As outlined in the RNO-G whitepaper, this `low-band' trigger is the primary driver for the array's trigger-level sensitivity to deep neutrinos~\cite{RNO-G:2020rmc .}
elements.sent-2411-276: The Flower trigger is a flexible design that allows for updating the FPGA algorithms, as applied to the streaming data, remotely.
elements.sent-2411-277: The design goal is to build a phased-array trigger on these compact 4~Vpol antennas within the FPGA, as demonstrated previously in ARA~\cite{Allison:2018ynt , and was first deployed on the Flower during the 2024 season.}
elements.sent-2411-278: For the first few deployment seasons, the as-implemented Flower trigger in the RNO-G-7 array comprised a simpler trigger algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-279: This initial trigger imposes a high-low requirement on each signal channel within a tight time window, which identifies for bipolar impulsive signals similar to the trigger described here~\cite{Kleinfelder57371 , but in the digital domain.}
elements.sent-2411-280: Once a trigger decision is formed, an external trigger signal is sent to the Radiant to initiate a full-station event readout of the `full-band' waveform as shown in~\autoref{fig:trigger_block .}
elements.sent-2411-281: The performance of this trigger is discussed in~\autoref{sec:trig_performance .}
elements.sent-2411-282: Each station contains a modular Station Controller board which is responsible for interfacing with all the subsystems and eventually transmitting data off of the local station.
elements.sent-2411-283: One of the primary functions of the Station Controller is to act as a carrier for the BeagleBone Black Industrial~\cite{BBB single board computer (SBC), which is responsible for science data collection from the Radiant board.}
elements.sent-2411-284: Additionally, the Station Controller incorporates a Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS) daughterboard primarily for timing, the wireless communication systems (\autoref{sec:comms ), and a microcontroller (Microchip SAMD21J18A) responsible for managing the controller board, including collecting housekeeping sensor data and turning subsystem power rails on and off.}
elements.sent-2411-285: The SBC runs a Debian Linux operating system, which is built using a custom RNO-G image.
elements.sent-2411-286: It has Serial Peripheral Interface (SPI) and Universal Asynchronous Receiver Transmitter (UART) links to the Radiant, for data transfer and slow control, respectively, and an SPI link to the Flower board (see \autoref{sec:trigger ).}
elements.sent-2411-287: Software on the SBC is used to configure the digitizer boards for data taking, and then perform data acquisition.
elements.sent-2411-288: After initial configuration (including tuning of LAB4D parameters), the data acquisition daemon (rno-g-acq) is started, which is fully steered by configuration files.
elements.sent-2411-289: In addition to recording the data and writing it compressed to disk, rno-g-acq is also responsible for dynamically adjusting trigger thresholds to attempt to maintain an approximately constant rate.
elements.sent-2411-290: Standard runs are 2 hours in length, although shorter runs may be taken during calibration campaigns or while tuning configuration parameters.
elements.sent-2411-291: The SBC has a 4 GB embedded Multimedia Card (eMMC) used for the operating system and other programs and a \SI{128 {GB industrial SD card to temporarily store data.}}
elements.sent-2411-292: Data is written out in a simple custom binary format and compressed using zlib.
elements.sent-2411-293: Due to a lack of random access, this format is not convenient for analysis so data is typically converted to ROOT format \cite{ROOT after leaving the detector.}
elements.sent-2411-294: The GNSS receiver is a single-band u-blox LEA-M8T module (upgraded to a dual-band u-blox ZED-F9T in 2024) and is enabled via a general purpose input-output (GPIO) from the SBC, and also has a UART connection to the SBC for configuration and readout.
elements.sent-2411-295: The pulse-per-second (PPS) pulse from the GNSS receiver is distributed to the Radiant and Flower boards, the calibration driver board, and (in newer revisions) the SBC itself as common timing reference signal across the RNO-G array.
elements.sent-2411-296: Software on the SBC is used to configure the GNSS receiver and can also record GNSS data which can be used, for example, for GNSS reflectometry to measure the ice height \cite{Komjathy2000 .}
elements.sent-2411-297: The SBC controls and communicates to a number of other peripherals.
elements.sent-2411-298: The single USB link on the SBC isx used to transmit and receive data from the LTE modem (\autoref{sec:comms ), and a USB2.0 4-port hub chip is employed on a newer controller board revision that allows for additional USB interfaces, such as industrial-rated flash drives for redundant storage.}
elements.sent-2411-299: The SBC also controls the calibration transmit board, using a GPIO to turn it on and I2C GPIO expanders control board functionality.
elements.sent-2411-300: The SBC furthermore has a UART connection to the controller board microcontroller, which can be used to request sensor data, turn power rails on and off, or in-situ program the microcontroller.
elements.sent-2411-301: The microcontroller firmware is designed to robustly operate the station even with the SBC off (as well as enabling power cycles of the SBC).
elements.sent-2411-302: It incorporates a LoRaWAN communications stack for remote control independent of the SBC and can also be configured to automatically turn the station into low power mode when battery voltage falls below a configurable threshold.
elements.sent-2411-303: External SPI flash memory holds configuration parameters, as well as four slots for microcontroller firmware.
elements.sent-2411-304: The custom bootloader for the microcontroller can be instructed to load any of those 4 slots on a reboot, allowing for safe in-situ reprogramming of microcontroller functionality.
elements.sent-2411-305: A special low-power mode designed for winter operation turns off most devices (\autoref{sec:power_stuff ) and causes the microcontroller to sleep most of the time.}
elements.sent-2411-306: RNO-G stations are not grid-connected and must generate their own power from renewable systems.
elements.sent-2411-307: The first seven stations are primarily solar-powered, with wind-generation options undergoing development and testing in order to prolong operations into the polar night present at RNO-G's latitude.
elements.figure-2411-016: \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{figures/power_23.pdf} \caption{Performance of the solar powered system of RNO-G. Top: Average photovoltaic current as function of date and UTC hour at one RNO-G station. Clearly visible are the effects of the position of the Sun throughout the year. Bottom: The same figure for the average battery voltage.} \label{fig:solar_power}
elements.sent-2411-308: The solar power system consists of two solar panels delivering up to 350 W.
elements.sent-2411-309: The solar panels are connected through Electromagnetic Interference (EMI) filter pins to a Flexcharge NC25A solar regulator with significant polar plateau heritage.
elements.sent-2411-310: A battery bank comprised of four sealed-lead-acid batteries (DEKA 8G31, 12V 108Ah) provides approximately 5 kWh of battery capacity, which practically de-rates by a factor of 2-3 at typical operating temperatures and to avoid deep-discharge cycles.
elements.sent-2411-311: The first 2021-deployed stations were built with 12~V battery banks, while the later 2022 stations were wired at 24~V to decrease the charging currents and for improved compatibility with potential future wind turbines.
elements.sent-2411-312: There is a custom power filtering and routing board within the instrument box called the `TidyPower', which interfaces to the solar panels and batteries, simplifying the internal cabling.
elements.sent-2411-313: The solar panels are connected to the instrument using a pair of shielded four-conductor 14AWG cables, with lengths up to 10~m, to accommodate maximal charge currents of 13~A (25~A) for the 24~V (12~V) systems.
elements.sent-2411-314: The RNO-G controller board includes sensing circuits to record the solar panel voltage and charge current, as well as to monitor the battery voltage and temperature.
elements.sent-2411-315: The RNO-G instrument uses a pair of wide-voltage input (9-36~V) and high-efficiency (>90$\%$) step-down DC-DC converters to generate the internal voltage rails used by the instrument subsystems from the primary battery voltage.
elements.sent-2411-316: An input-side EMI filter circuit is implemented before these DC-DC switching converters that sufficiently rejects undesired radio emissions that might be picked up in our receivers.
elements.sent-2411-317: A dedicated +3.3 V micropower DC-DC converter is used to power the controller board microcontroller, LoRaWAN wireless transceiver, and low-level controller board sensors.
elements.sent-2411-318: This +3.3V rail is always enabled, providing power to the microcontroller and its peripherals used to continuously monitor the system, provide resets if necessary, and run in the over-winter ultra-low power mode.
elements.sent-2411-319: A separate high-current +5V DC-DC converter is used to power everything else in the instrument, including the digitizer boards, amplifiers, SBC, and LTE modem.
elements.sent-2411-320: This converter can provide up to 8~A, and can be remotely enabled and disabled by the microcontroller via LoRaWAN.
elements.sent-2411-321: All downstream RF amplifier boards have a local low-dropout regulator and additional input filtering, which suppress any residual power-supply noise on these sensitive components from this primary +5V rail.
elements.sent-2411-322: In low-power mode, the RNO-G instrument draws less than 100mW, allowing the station microcontroller to remain on and transmitting low-duty cycle station monitoring information throughout the polar winter period.
elements.sent-2411-323: The primary power draw in winter-mode is the self-consumption of the Flexcharge solar controller ($\sim$3~mA).
elements.sent-2411-324: We note that all stations in the RNO-G-7 array have successfully operated in this mode over at least two winters without a failure, re-engaging to full-power science operations in the spring.
elements.sent-2411-325: Significant effort is now being made to develop a wind power system that has minimal in-band EMI emissions and is robust to the extreme weather conditions at Summit Station.
elements.sent-2411-326: With a successful development of these new wind turbine systems, we expect to be able operate during 70-90\% of the year given the recorded wind conditions at Summit.
elements.sent-2411-327: RNO-G uses a private LTE cellular network for science data transfer and remote access to the SBC.
elements.sent-2411-328: LTE was chosen to reduce the power requirements at each station and avoid use of highly directional antennas that may become misaligned during storms.
elements.sent-2411-329: The LTE network operates on LTE band 8 (uplink \SI{880 -\SI{915 {MHz , downlink \SI{925 -\SI{960 {MHz ), currently using one eNodeB on the lowest \SI{10 {MHz channels, though additional eNodeB's may be added in the future to increase capacity.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-330: The LTE backend is run on a virtual machine on the RNO-G Summit Station Central Server, a Dell PowerEdge 7515 2U rack-mounted server.
elements.sent-2411-331: The LTE system was procured from Star Solutions.
elements.sent-2411-332: Two large 13 dBi base station antennas were installed on top of the Big House, the main building at Summit Station, that send and receive the LTE communications to/from the RNO-G sites.
elements.figure-2411-017: \includegraphics[width=0.60\textwidth]{figures/comms.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.38\textwidth]{figures/survey.pdf} \caption{Performance of the communications systems of RNO-G. Left: Received signal strength, defined by the Reference Signal Received Power (RSRP), as function of time. The LTE network is generally turned off during the winter, as long as wind power is not available. Right: Received signal strength as function of distance to the station. Data are taken from a survey conducted during the first installation season. The color scale depicts the effects of moving out of the field-of-view of the base antenna.} \label{fig:rssi}
elements.sent-2411-333: Each station has an 8 dBi omnidirectional \SI{900 {MHz antenna (LPRS-ANT-868) mounted on a solar panel.}}
elements.sent-2411-334: This is connected via a 15~m LMR240 coaxial cable through a \SI{900 {MHz ceramic resonator bandpass filter (MiniCircuits CSBP-A940+) to a Nimbelink NL-SW-LTE-TC4EU cellular modem, which communicates with the SBC over USB.}}
elements.sent-2411-335: The modem also has a UART connection to the microcontroller, used for monitoring the LTE connection quality.
elements.sent-2411-336: A software daemon running on the SBC verifies connectivity and automatically power cycles the modem if connectivity is lost.
elements.sent-2411-337: The LTE network performance is summarized in \autoref{fig:rssi .}
elements.sent-2411-338: Generally, performance is stable, though susceptible to strong snow storms.
elements.sent-2411-339: A post-deployment survey indicates that the LTE network indeed services the entire 35-station array.
elements.sent-2411-340: The design requirement was to provide sufficient bandwidth for a sustained data rate of 1 Hz.
elements.sent-2411-341: A LoRaWAN~\cite{lorawanspec network on the \SI{915 {MHz ISM band was also deployed at Summit for monitoring of sensor data collected by the controller board as well as remote control of the microcontroller via predetermined commands.}}}
elements.sent-2411-342: This second network is required because the LTE modem uses too much power to be enabled in low-power mode.
elements.sent-2411-343: LoRaWAN uses much less power but is also only capable of very limited bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-344: Sensor data (temperatures, currents, voltages) are sent once a minute during normal operation and once an hour while in low-power mode.
elements.sent-2411-345: Additionally, LTE and LoRaWAN connectivity data are also sent.
elements.sent-2411-346: Using LoRaWAN commands, we can put the stations into and out of low-power mode, power cycle the LTE modem and SBC, reset the microcontroller, or turn a small heater on or off.
elements.sent-2411-347: The Summit Central Server collects all data from both LTE and LoRaWAN.
elements.sent-2411-348: Science data from LTE is converted to ROOT format and stored on redundant disks.
elements.sent-2411-349: A random subset is sent via satellite to RNO-G insitutions for monitoring purposes.
elements.sent-2411-350: The full dataset is transferred by hand.
elements.sent-2411-351: In the future, online analysis will run on the server in order to generate multimessenger alerts.
elements.sent-2411-352: Sensor data from LoRaWAN as well as other types of monitoring data (local weather station, server, network and UPS housekeeping) are inserted into a postgresql database, which is then replicated off-Greenland.
elements.sent-2411-353: The RNO-G-7 instrument box, shown in \autoref{fig:rnog-box , holds all of the digital, power, and surface-side RF electronics described in this section within a single RF-shielded enclosure.}
elements.sent-2411-354: The custom 24"x16"x8" enclosure is machined from aluminum and subsequently applied with a chemical-conversion coating that enhances the conductivity for RF shielding purposes.
elements.sent-2411-355: All external I/O connectors are all placed on a single side of the box for ease of cable management and installation within the environmental enclosure in the field.
elements.sent-2411-356: This tightly integrated design enables the scalable production of the RNO-G instrument.
elements.figure-2411-018: \includegraphics[width=0.6\textwidth]{figures/rnog-instrument-annoted.jpg} \caption{The RNO-G instrument box with the lid removed, exposing the internal electronics. A single Faraday enclosure houses all of the surface-side electronic subsystems described in this section. } \label{fig:rnog-box}
elements.sent-2411-357: All hardware components are cold-soaked and temperature tested in the lab before installation.
elements.sent-2411-358: Furthermore, all relevant performance characteristics are measured (e.g.\ S-parameters, pulser waveforms) before shipping to Greenland.
elements.sent-2411-359: The calibration measurements are stored in a database (MongoDB, see \autoref{fig:dbs , right), which is directly accessible for reconstruction and detector simulations.}
elements.sent-2411-360: The open source code NuRadioMC~\cite{Glaser:2019cws is used for reconstruction in RNO-G, with only RNO-G-specific modules being private to the collaboration.}
elements.figure-2411-019: \includegraphics[width=0.55\textwidth]{figures/Graphana_v1.png} \includegraphics[width=0.44\textwidth,trim={13cm 1cm 1cm 1cm },clip]{figures/Detector_DB_v1.png} \caption{Database interfaces for the operation, monitoring and calibration of RNO-G. elements.sent-2411-361: The grafana package is used to visualize the postgresql database containing sensor data from stations, with instances operating both in Greenland (for the deployment team as well as to generate near-realtime alerts) and down South (for general monitoring and historical inspection.) A set of rich grafana dashboards have been created to help visualize the state of the detector (\autoref{fig:dbs , left).}
elements.sent-2411-362: Alerts of housekeeping parameters deviating from expected values can be relayed by e-mail or Slack.
elements.sent-2411-363: The installation of the science instrumentation at a new RNO-G station is a 3-4 day process, involving string deployment, near-surface antenna installation, construction of the solar-power and communications structures, and the early validation of performance.
elements.sent-2411-364: Initially, the instrument box is installed in an insulation-lined environmental enclosure with the battery bank, and wired to the solar panels and communications antennas.
elements.sent-2411-365: The LTE and LoRaWAN links are then verified, bringing the instrument online.
elements.sent-2411-366: The environmental enclosure and solar panel installations are shown in~\autoref{fig:installation .}
elements.figure-2411-020: \includegraphics[width=0.49\textwidth]{figures/vault.png} \includegraphics[width=0.49\textwidth]{figures/installation.png} \caption{Fielded RNO-G station.
