On the right, the station-site is shown shortly after completing the installation with the central vault flanked by two south-facing vertically-mounted solar panels. The communications and GPS antennas are mounted on the top of these structures.} \label{fig:installation}
elements.sent-2411-367: The antenna installation then follows, concurrently deploying a borehole string and the corresponding 3-LPDA trench along the same spoke in the station layout.
elements.sent-2411-368: Each string and set of surface antennas is connected to the instrument immediately after deployment to verify channel-level operation and observation of thermal-noise backgrounds.
elements.sent-2411-369: In the case a bad channel is found, the deployment team can consider raising the string to repair or replace, and a procedure for this action has been defined and implemented in the field with good success.
elements.sent-2411-370: The helper string takes $\sim$3~hours to deploy, while the power string takes 5-6~hours due to the added complexity of the upper Vpol antennas, which require more complicated load-transfer operations and cable-management in the string deployment.
elements.sent-2411-371: A 3/8" diameter poly rope is used for rigging the borehole strings, which is loaded from the borehole instrumentation at significantly less (<10$\times$) than its 2,400~lb breaking strength rating.
elements.sent-2411-372: This section describes the initial performance of the first 7 stations of RNO-G.
elements.sent-2411-373: We will highlight measurements taken during installation and those taken with early science data.
elements.sent-2411-374: These data are used by RNO-G to develop methods and tools to reliably operate and calibrate the detector.
elements.sent-2411-375: The livetime of the RNO-G-7 array is shown in \autoref{fig:uptime , which notes the station deployment date and distinguishes the operational modes including science data taking.}
elements.sent-2411-376: The best performing station (13) delivered a science data-taking uptime of 50.5\% and an overall uptime (SBC on) of 75.1\%.
elements.sent-2411-377: The SBC on time is relatively similar for all stations, meaning that the power supply has been working reliably under conservative operation parameters.
elements.sent-2411-378: Overall, RNO-G has 4 stations that delivered science data for more than 40\% of the year.
elements.figure-2411-021: \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{figures/uptime.pdf} \caption{Uptime of the stations as function of time, spanning the three summer seasons of 2021 to 2023. It should be noted that prototype wind turbines were tested at two stations (12, 22), which impacted science data taking.} \label{fig:uptime}
elements.sent-2411-379: At the end of fall, the digitizers are turned off first to ensure that the stations enter winter with full batteries.
elements.sent-2411-380: In spring, the stations are slowly turned on to not immediately drain the batteries again and to start warming up the stations.
elements.sent-2411-381: This is needed to avoid booting the DAQ hardware at or below $-40^{\circ}$C, where performance is less reliable.
elements.sent-2411-382: It is anticipated that with optimized processes and improved experience with the system, the science data-taking time can be increased.

elements.sent-2411-383: The time-domain response of the RNO-G system predominantly determines the characteristics of the received Askaryan signal at the instrument, and has implications for trigger design and reconstruction capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-384: These responses have been measured for each component in the lab, and at a full-system level using both fielded data from in-situ calibration pulsing, using the transmitting antennas on the helper strings, and lab data taken in anechoic chamber measurements.
elements.sent-2411-385: Additional in-situ system-timing performance metrics are presented in this section, including the system timing resolution and the reconstruction of vertical pulser scans of the power string receivers.
elements.sent-2411-386: The on-boresight phase response of the Vpol receiving antenna RVEL (the amplitude response is described in~\autoref{fig:vpol_display ) is shown on the left, comparing a measurement in the lab to simulations in-air and within an in-ice borehole.}
elements.sent-2411-387: There is mostly good agreement with some discrepancy between measurement and simulation after the main pulse cycle, which might be attributed to undamped reflections in the anechoic chamber measurement.
elements.sent-2411-388: The impulse response of the full electronics chain, including the interconnected RF and DAQ components, is shown in the middle graphic.
elements.sent-2411-389: By design, this response has minimal group-delay dispersion with most of the signal power present within the first 10~ns, although the ringing in the pulse tail is driven by the dispersion in the high-pass filter edge.
elements.sent-2411-390: On the right, the impulse waveform provided by the calibration pulser system is shown, as presented to the transmitting Vpol antenna, at a mid-range attenuation setting.
elements.sent-2411-391: The time-domain response of the full system is examined in ~\autoref{fig:sysresponse2 , using the in situ calibration pulser and simulated Askaryan signals.}
elements.sent-2411-392: In the left frame, an average pulser waveform on station 11 is compared to an expected waveform that is generated by cascading the component-level responses in~\autoref{fig:sysresponse1 .}
elements.sent-2411-393: The expected (amplitude-normalized) waveform is generated using the formulation outlined in~\cite{Barwick:2014boa .}
elements.sent-2411-394: We find a peak cross-correlation coefficient of 0.94 between the measured and modeled received pulser waveforms, suggesting a good understanding of the time-domain system response.
elements.sent-2411-395: Askaryan electric-field signals are simulated at two off-cone view angles and sent through the receiver system response in the middle plot.
elements.sent-2411-396: This shows the expected relative enhancement of the low-frequency tail of the received waveform for more off-cone events.
elements.sent-2411-397: In the right frame, the Flower trigger-band pulse spectra are shown for simulated Askaryan signals received at a variety of off-cone angles, compared to the pulse spectrum from events provided by the in situ calibration system.
elements.sent-2411-398: Although the calibration pulser provides an impulse that is fairly well-matched to an on-cone Askaryan signal, the marginally higher turn-on frequency for received waveforms from the calibration pulser has implications when evaluating the trigger using these events, as will be discussed in~\autoref{sec:trig_performance .}
elements.sent-2411-399: The relative timing resolution of an RNO-G station can be measured using the calibration system as shown in \autoref{fig:timing-resolution .}
elements.sent-2411-400: The timing resolution of the system depends on the received SNR, which can be varied at the transmitter as implemented for the trigger evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-401: Timing is measured by cross-correlating the pulses received by two adjacent Vpol antennas on a helper string opposite the pulsing string.

elements.sent-2411-404: For clarity, we define the SNR metric used for evaluating the RNO-G timing and trigger performance.
elements.sent-2411-405: The SNR is defined as the maximum peak-to-peak amplitude divided by twice the standard deviation of pure noise $V_\text{noise rms}$, which is the square root of the average of the squared deviations from the mean, i.e., \begin{equation}\label{eq:snr} \text{SNR} = \frac{V_\text{pp}}{2 \cdot V_\text{noise rms}} = \frac{V_\text{pp}}{2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{n_\text{noise}} \sum^{n_\text{noise}}_{i=1} (V_i - \bar{V})^2}}, \ \text{with} \ \bar{V} = \frac{1}{n_\text{noise}} \sum^{n_\text{noise}}_{i=1} V_i.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-406: The index $i$ denotes the sample and $n_\text{noise}$ the number of samples of the considered noise.

elements.sent-2411-415: At the outset of RNO-G deployment, the Flower trigger was initially installed with a simple high-low coincidence requirement, which established a baseline trigger for science operations.
elements.sent-2411-416: To push towards higher sensitivity, an improved delay-and-sum beamforming trigger was deployed to the Flower during the summer of 2024 for first field testing.
elements.sent-2411-417: For both triggers, the Flower ADCs are gain-controlled to set the thermal noise V$_{\mathrm{rms }$ to 5~adu on each channel, to maintain equal trigger contributions from each Vpol receiver.}
elements.sent-2411-418: Both triggers have been evaluated in situ and are described here.
elements.sent-2411-419: The Flower board draws~2.7~W when operating the high-low coincidence trigger, and closer to 5~W when running the beamforming trigger.
elements.sent-2411-420: The simple coincidence trigger imparts a per-channel condition that requires the digital data to exceed a pair of symmetric high- (above noise baseline) and low- (below noise baseline) amplitude thresholds within a $\sim$14~ns window.
elements.sent-2411-421: A system-level trigger is subsequently defined if a 2-of-4 condition is met within a 42 ns coincidence window.
elements.sent-2411-422: These trigger conditions are configurable in the DAQ.
elements.sent-2411-423: The symmetric positive and negative thresholds are controlled by software, as shown in \autoref{fig:threshold_stability for station 13, and are set so that the resulting deep-antenna trigger rate is $\sim1$~Hz.}
elements.sent-2411-424: The noise-riding thresholds are found to be stable for the deep phased-array Vpols.
elements.sent-2411-425: Each channel's trigger thresholds are dynamically adjusted based on the single-channel trigger rate at a lower ``servo" threshold, typically set to 80\% of the trigger threshold.
elements.sent-2411-426: The servo threshold rate is substantially higher than when noise-riding, allowing for more rapid feedback on the trigger threshold.
elements.sent-2411-427: The firmware records the rate at the servo threshold each second, and that is compared with a rate goal to determine if the threshold should be modified.
elements.sent-2411-428: The firmware for the delay-and-sum beamforming trigger is based on the design of the trigger system first deployed on the ARA Phased Array \cite{Allison:2018ynt , although with some distinct features for the $Flower$ architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-429: Due to the lower Flower sampling rate, a first stage of 4$\times$ upsampling is used to generate sufficient delay-steps to have full beam coverage over the elevation angle range-of-interest.
elements.sent-2411-430: A finite-impulse response (FIR) upsampling approach is implemented, using a 31-coefficient low-pass filter with a critical frequency at \SI{238 {MHz .}}
elements.sent-2411-431: After upsampling, 12 delay-and-sum beams are formed, which are constructed on a plane-wave signal hypothesis and are evenly spaced to cover an elevation angle range of -60$^{\circ}$ to +60$^{\circ}$.
elements.sent-2411-432: Each beam operates as an independent trigger channel, with a dedicated threshold and servo control via software.
elements.sent-2411-433: The threshold criterion for each beam is computed using a power integration within a 16.9~ns window, which is re-evaluated at $\sim$4.2~ns intervals.

elements.figure-2411-028: \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{figures/deep_trigger_new.pdf} \caption{Measured and simulated efficiency curves for the \emph{Flower trigger}. elements.sent-2411-518: We have reported the station and instrument design of the first seven stations of RNO-G, and highlighted the performance in the first 3 seasons at Summit Station in Greenland.
elements.sent-2411-519: The RNO-G stations have delivered a livetime as expected, solely operating on solar-power, and have commenced collecting scientific datasets to be used for UHE neutrino searches.
elements.sent-2411-520: The design and performance of the initial RNO-G instrument is established.
elements.sent-2411-521: The noise environment and trigger thresholds at the phased-array antennas on the power string have excellent stability.
elements.sent-2411-522: While the array has been operating primarily using a simplified coincidence trigger, the newly-deployed beamforming trigger demonstrates a sensitivity closer to the target design originally outlined in the whitepaper.
elements.sent-2411-523: Several external signals (anthropogenic transmitters, the Sun, the Galaxy) have been measured with RNO-G in high quality, highlighting the performance of the instrument and the RF suitability of the site near Summit Station.
elements.sent-2411-524: The first seven installed stations significantly enhanced the technology readiness for a number of new custom instrumental systems, deployed into harsh polar environment for the first time, including the new RF signal chains, borehole string design, and winter-power operational mode (to name a few).
elements.sent-2411-525: RNO-G-7 was built and delivered with production-scale efforts.
elements.sent-2411-526: RNO-G is the first large-scale implementation of the in-ice radio neutrino technique and important lessons have been learned in the first years.
elements.sent-2411-527: New aspects of the RNO-G instrument architecture are currently under test or exist as conceptual design, including the integration of micro wind-turbines, to enable science operation over-winter, and new DAQ systems, so that the instrument can operate with even higher reliability and autonomy.
elements.sent-2411-528: For a large-scale array of distributed stations, it is essential to reduce the need of user intervention for station operations.
elements.sent-2411-529: We are also improving the design of the above-surface structures that house the solar panels and communications antenna, to reduce yearly maintenance as we better adapt to the high accumulation and snow-drift rates on the Greenland ice sheet.
elements.sent-2411-530: Once the 35-station array is completed, the collaboration is considering several expansion scenarios, such as enhancing the air shower capabilities by adding infill stations or further expanding the array.

elements.sent-2411-537: With GNO on indefinite hiatus, a new experimental effort to build a large-scale radio array reemerged in the form of the Radio Neutrino Observatory Greenland (RNO-G), which was first described in a 2021 whitepaper~\cite{RNO-G:2020rmc .}
elements.sent-2411-538: The RNO-G work effort began in earnest in mid-2019, when design and construction of 10~full RNO-G stations of hardware components was initiated, followed by field deployments to Greenland in 2021 and 2022 during which time the initial seven stations were installed and commissioned.
elements.sent-2411-539: The square grid has an inter-station spacing of \SI{1.25 {km , which was chosen so that the effective neutrino-detection volume of each station is independent in the lower energy range ($<$300~PeV) and leads to inter-station coincident measurements at the 10\% level for neutrino interactions at the EeV energy scale, based on simulation studies in the design phase of the project~\cite{RNO-G:2020rmc .}}}
elements.sent-2411-540: The stations are denoted by a grid numbering scheme (XY) and, colloquially, by fauna endemic to Greenland, using the local language kalaallisut.
elements.sent-2411-541: The first seven installed stations of RNO-G (RNO-G-7) are Nanoq (Polar Bear, 11), Terianniaq (Arctic Fox, 12), Ukaleq (Arctic Hare, 13), Amaroq (Arctic Wolf, 21), Avinngaq (Arctic Lemming, 22), Ukaliatsiaq (Stoat, 23), and Qappik (Wolverine, 24).
elements.sent-2411-542: The layout plan of the RNO-G station is shown on the right in \autoref{fig:array_and_station_layout , which emerged from design studies presented in the whitepaper~\cite{RNO-G:2020rmc .}}
elements.sent-2411-543: Each station is composed of 24 low-noise receiving antennas installed in a hybrid configuration with both near-surface and deep receivers.
elements.sent-2411-544: In all polar ice, the density of the ice increases with depth within the firn ($\sim$80~m near Summit).
elements.sent-2411-545: It follows that radio waves from a deep source, such as the Askaryan-generated emission from an UHE neutrino, propagate along curved ray paths as they approach the surface so that antennas deployed deeper in the ice are sensitive to a larger volume of ice.
elements.sent-2411-546: For the RNO-G station, the linear polarization angle of the Askaryan signal is used, in part, to reconstruct the neutrino arrival direction, so both the vertical and horizontal components of the electric field are measured with the borehole antennas.
elements.sent-2411-547: Due to the signal propagation effects described above, the main neutrino effective volume arises from the closely packed triggering subarray of four antennas at the bottom of what is called the power string.
elements.sent-2411-548: The trigger threshold on this subarray largely determines the neutrino detection sensitivity of an RNO-G station.
elements.sent-2411-549: This trigger threshold is defined as a transient signal-to-noise ratio, which relates the received signal voltage magnitude to the combined system and environmental noise, as discussed in~\autoref{sec:performance .}
elements.sent-2411-550: The distributed antennas on the power string have long vertical baselines, which drive the energy resolution obtainable with RNO-G \cite{Aguilar:2021uzt .}
elements.sent-2411-551: The two helper strings provide horizontal baselines for both deep-receiver polarizations, which are particularly needed for reconstructing the neutrino arrival direction.
elements.sent-2411-552: Combined, the three borehole strings form an equilateral triangle with horizontal baselines of $\sim$\SI{35.5 {m equipped with deep sub-arrays of antennas.}}
elements.sent-2411-553: These provide the baselines for time-domain pulse interferometry that are sufficient for sub-degree azimuth and zenith reconstruction of the impinging radio signal.~\cite{Plaisier:2023cxz .}
elements.sent-2411-554: In general, the neutrino reconstruction will depend on the details of the event and, in most cases, will be limited by the measurement of the polarization angle~\cite{Bouma:2023koi .}
elements.sent-2411-555: The near-surface antennas are connected to a dedicated receiver and trigger block, which provides both additional neutrino effective volume and a tag for air shower backgrounds \cite{DeKockere:2022bto,Pyras:2023crm .}
elements.sent-2411-556: All of the receiver channels are connected to one instrument box that contains the data acquisition (DAQ) system, and provides power and the wireless communication links (\autoref{sec:instrument ).}
elements.sent-2411-557: As the initial stations comprising RNO-G-7 are run solely through solar power, we required a station power budget of about 25~W to allow for science operations at least 50$\%$ of the year.
elements.sent-2411-558: The station design also required a low-power hibernation mode (<50~mW), in order to preserve the batteries during the polar night.
elements.sent-2411-559: The drill-sonde is suspended on a cable, traversing vertically in the hole as controlled by a custom-designed winch system from MacArtney.
elements.sent-2411-560: The incremental drilling-depth per run depends on the structure of the ice and how quickly the chips fill the drill barrel.
elements.sent-2411-561: This typically decreases with depth, with drill runs of $\sim$2m common at the onset and runs of $\sim$1-1.5m at depth.
elements.sent-2411-562: The boreholes are extremely vertical due to the long length of the drill-sonde, with small deviations of $<$1$^\circ$ measured with initial inclinometer data.
elements.sent-2411-563: More systematic studies of the boreholes will be performed in upcoming seasons, as inclinometers were directly added to the drill sonde in 2024.

elements.sent-2411-566: The RF system was designed to balance the small power budget (<6~W for 24~channels) with the low-noise (<120~K noise temperature) requirements, and to fit within small and high-density form factors.
elements.sent-2411-567: The deep borehole receiving antennas are equipped with a custom RF-over-fiber (RFoF) implementation for long-distance signal transport, which was the favored solution over long coaxial cable runs in order maintain a $\sim$flat ($\pm$2~dB) gain profile without a significant frequency-dependent slope and to have a manageable borehole-string design.
elements.sent-2411-568: The In-ice Gain with Low-noise Unit (IGLU) is the front-end RF module that is installed directly above each borehole antenna, and includes a 50~$\Omega$-matched low-noise amplifier chip and custom amplitude-modulated RFoF transmitter circuit.
elements.sent-2411-569: The IGLU draws a total of 43~mA on a 3.3V supply voltage, meeting the ultra-low power requirement of a fraction of a Watt per channel.
elements.sent-2411-570: The surface antenna receivers, called the SURFACE boards, are custom highly-integrated circuit boards that connect directly to the LPDA antennas via 10~m coaxial cables (LMR400).
elements.sent-2411-571: The gain profile of the RF system has three major requirements: 1) boost the baseline antenna voltage-output signal to a measurable level of at least 5-10~mV$_{\mathrm{rms }$, 2) preserve a linear response for signals with amplitudes at or greater than $30\times$ above the thermal noise level, and 3) sufficiently reject out-of-band noise, such as the wireless communications signals that sit just above 850~MHz.}
elements.sent-2411-572: The gain and input return loss responses of both the downhole and surface signal chains are shown in \autoref{fig:chain_forward_gain .}
elements.sent-2411-573: The downhole chain has a return loss that exceeds 10~dB (2:1 VSWR) from 110-720~MHz, and the surface chain meets the same performance over a 70-720~MHz bandwidth.

elements.sent-2411-620: The time-difference resolution of an RNO-G station can be measured using the calibration system as shown in \autoref{fig:timing-resolution .}
elements.sent-2411-621: The time-difference resolution of the system depends on the received SNR, which can be varied at the transmitter as implemented for the trigger evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-622: To measure this timing resolution, we study the pulses received by two adjacent Vpol antennas on a helper string opposite the pulsing string.
elements.sent-2411-623: For each SNR step, the waveforms of each event are processed by upsampling to a~5~ps grid and the time difference is measured by finding the time of maximal cross-correlation between these upsampled pulses.
elements.sent-2411-624: The statistical time-difference resolution is defined as the 68\% containment interval on the dataset taken at each SNR.
elements.sent-2411-625: For high SNR ($>$10) signals, the time-difference resolution is found to be 10-20~ps, which characterizes the timing resolution inherent to the RNO-G instrument.
elements.sent-2411-626: At lower SNR values, this timing error increases to 80-90~ps for signals near the trigger threshold level.

elements.sent-2411-633: As shown in~\cite{RNO-G2024solar , the absolute pointing of the RNO-G stations to the incoming radio signal direction can be calibrated to half a degree, which exceeds the design goals, as the expected angular resolution for neutrino detection will be dominated by signal parameters other than the absolute signal arrival direction \cite{Plaisier:2023cxz .}}
elements.sent-2411-634: Detailed measurements of antenna depth are recorded by the field team during string deployment, which is followed by a measurement of the locations of the surface components and boreholes using GNSS surveying tools with real-time kinematic positioning precision.
elements.sent-2411-635: We have reported the station and instrument design of the RNO-G-7, and highlighted the performance in the first 3 seasons at Summit Station in Greenland.
elements.sent-2411-636: The RNO-G stations have demonstrated livetimes that are close to design expectations (50\%) while operating on solar-power, and have commenced collecting scientific datasets to be used for UHE neutrino searches.
elements.sent-2411-637: The noise environment and trigger thresholds at the phased-array antennas on the power string have excellent stability, which meet the design goals of the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-638: While the array has been operating primarily using a simplified coincidence trigger, the newly-deployed beamforming trigger demonstrates a threshold at the 2.9-3.4~SNR level, depending on the off-cone angle of the Askaryan signal.
elements.sent-2411-639: This trigger performance does not reach the optimistic 2.0~SNR threshold design target that was originally outlined in the whitepaper, however, further algorithm development is underway to push closer towards this goal.
elements.sent-2411-640: The RNO-G-7 installed stations significantly enhanced the technology readiness for a number of new custom instrumental systems, deployed into harsh polar environment for the first time, including the new RF signal chains, borehole string design, and winter-power operational mode.
